ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific node for packaged ready-to-drink mocha; 'coffee beverage' (FOODON_03301099) is the closest defensible parent class. A more specific node for RTD mocha does not exist in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP covers coffee-based beverages broadly. No specific code exists for packaged RTD mocha with chocolate and milk; this is the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept identified for packaged ready-to-drink mocha coffee beverage as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single-serve packaged mocha is one can or bottle of approximately 240 ml, equivalent to about 240 g, providing roughly 139 kcal per serving.

nutrient_source.title: Mocha (packaged) — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for packaged mocha (cr_id D09010047). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this record with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for canned mocha coffee beverage (NDB No. 14209) as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown; this is documented as an adapted value.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for packaged RTD mocha in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 53 is estimated using the closest analogous food: sweetened milk coffee beverage (GI ~53) from Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281-2283). Per the standard GI classification scale (low: ≤55, medium: 56–69, high: ≥70), a GI of 53 falls in the low category. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (53) × available carbohydrate per serving (21.7 g for 240 g serving) / 100 = 11.5. This is an analogous estimate and should be replaced with a direct measurement when available.

hypertension.key_factor: High added sugar content (8.43 g/100 g) independently raises blood pressure risk; sodium is low at 49 mg/100 g but does not offset the sugar burden
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid packaged mocha as a regular beverage; the 8.43 g sugar per 100 g qualifies it as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB), and habitual SSB consumption is independently associated with elevated blood pressure per AHA 2018 guidelines, regardless of its low sodium content.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 8.43 g sugar/100 g and only 0.09 g dietary fibre/100 g; liquid sugar causes rapid postprandial glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: Packaged mocha should be avoided by individuals with type 2 diabetes; as a sugar-sweetened beverage delivering 8.43 g sugar per 100 g with negligible fibre (0.09 g/100 g), it causes rapid postprandial glucose elevation and qualifies as an SSB under ADA dietary guidelines, which recommend eliminating SSBs to improve glycaemic control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Added sugar content (8.43 g/100 g) and potential saturated fat from non-dairy creamer contribute to cardiovascular risk; estimated saturated fat ~0.9 g/100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should limit packaged mocha; the high added sugar content (8.43 g/100 g) contributes to hypertriglyceridaemia and endothelial dysfunction, and non-dairy creamer variants may add palm-based saturated fat, both of which are risk factors per ACC/AHA 2019 dietary guidelines.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High added sugar (8.43 g/100 g) with negligible fibre promotes pro-inflammatory mediators; minimal cocoa polyphenol content insufficient to offset sugar-driven inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid packaged mocha; the 8.43 g added sugar per 100 g promotes pro-inflammatory cytokine production, and the minimal cocoa content in a commercially processed RTD drink provides insufficient anti-inflammatory polyphenol benefit to offset this effect.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Packaged mocha (ready-to-drink) is a commercially manufactured, ultra-processed coffee beverage combining brewed coffee extract, cocoa powder, milk solids or non-dairy creamer, and added sugar. It is sold in cans or bottles and requires no preparation. The flavour profile is sweet, mildly chocolatey, and lightly coffee-forward, with a smooth, thin liquid texture. Per 100 g, it provides 58 kcal, 1.63 g protein, 1.38 g fat, 9.59 g carbohydrate, and 8.43 g sugar, with very low dietary fibre (0.09 g) and moderate sodium (49 mg). The high sugar-to-fibre ratio classifies it as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) under both ADA and AHA definitions. Clinically, it is unsuitable for individuals with type 2 diabetes due to rapid postprandial glucose elevation from liquid sugar with negligible fibre buffering. For hypertension, the added sugar independently raises blood pressure risk despite low sodium. For cardiovascular disease, habitual SSB consumption contributes to hypertriglyceridaemia and endothelial dysfunction. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 53 (low category, GI ≤55) based on analogous sweetened milk coffee beverages from Atkinson et al. 2008. It is widely consumed in Singapore and Southeast Asia as a breakfast or snack beverage. Common brands include Nescafé, Pokka, and Old Town. The product is typically halal-certified and vegetarian but not vegan due to milk solids.
common_user_queries: How much sugar is in a packaged mocha?
Is RTD mocha suitable for hypertension?
Calories in a can of mocha coffee
Is bottled mocha coffee high in sugar?
Can I drink mocha if I have high blood pressure?
Is canned mocha coffee good for weight loss?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (cr_id D09010047) with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is adapted from USDA FoodData Central for the closest equivalent RTD coffee beverage. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008. All primary nutrient data come from approved sources.
notes: calcium_mg is reported as 0.0 and iron_mg is reported as 0.0 in the HPB source record (cr_id D09010047); these are confirmed zero values per the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database and are not missing data. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 as these values are not reported in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database for this packaged mocha entry, and no supplementary approved source provides these values for this specific product category. Saturated fat (0.9 g/100 g) is adapted from USDA FoodData Central NDB No. 14209 (coffee, mocha, ready-to-drink) as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown; this adaptation is documented in nutrient_source. GI of 53 is an analogous estimate based on sweetened milk coffee beverage from Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care); per the standard GI classification scale (low ≤55, medium 56–69, high ≥70), GI 53 is correctly categorised as 'low'. A direct GI measurement for this specific product is not available in any approved GI source. The food is not a Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese dish by cuisine origin; it is a globally manufactured commercial product, so no mandatory local-language names apply under G6 multilingual rules. Dietary tags 'low_sodium' and 'low_calorie' from the input are preserved as contextual notes only; 'low_calorie' is borderline at 58 kcal/100 g for a beverage and should be reviewed against HPB's official nutrient claim thresholds.

Iteration 2: Corrected ontology match_type fields from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent'.

Updated gi_source notes and natural_language_description to reflect correct low GI category. Updated notes field to document the corrected GI categorisation.
